Lord Sen Shyamapada Mukherjee

Lord Sen, Shyamapada Mukherjee (National Institute of Technology, Rourkela)Expressive Power of QDRs February 20, 2026 1 / 19
Motivation
Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs) are central to near-term QML
Data reuploadingis widely used to enhance expressivity
Empirically effective — but:
Why does reuploading help?
How does expressivity scale with depth?
Does expressivity saturate?
This work provides atheoretical explanationusing Fourier analysis

Origin of Frequencies in QDRs
Each data-encoding unitary has the form
V(x) =e −ixH

Spectral decomposition gives:

X

e−ixλΠλ

Effect of Reuploading
For anR-reupload circuit:
U(x) =V(x)W R · · ·V(x)W1
Each encoding contributes one eigenvalue
A typical term accumulates phase:
e−ix(λ1+λ2+···+λR)
Hence all accumulated phases belong to:
α∈S (R) :=S+· · ·+S| {z }
Rtimes
Conclusion:Every term inU(x)carries a frequency fromS (R).
Lord Sen, Shyamapada Mukherjee (National Institute of Technology, Rourkela)Expressive Power of QDRs February 20, 2026 6 / 19
Why Frequencies Are Differences
The model output is:
f(x) =⟨ψ|U †(x)O U(x)|ψ⟩
U(x)contributese −ixα,α∈S (R)
U †(x)contributese +ixβ,β∈S (R)
e+ixβ e−ixα =e ix(β−α)
⇒ω=β−α∈S (R) −S (R)
Key insight:Frequencies arise asdifferences of combinatorial eigenvalue sums.
Lord Sen, Shyamapada Mukherjee (National Institute of Technology, Rourkela)Expressive Power of QDRs February 20, 2026 7 / 19
Why Sums Need Not Match: Example
UandU † are generated from the same Hamiltonian
Hence they share the same eigenvalue setσ(H)
But combinatorial sums can differ
Example:
σ(H) ={+1,−1}, R= 2
α= (+1) + (−1) = 0
β= (+1) + (+1) = 2
α̸=β⇒ω=β−α= 2
Conclusion:
ω∈S (R) −S (R)

Saturation of Expressivity
Key Theoretical Insight:
Frequency setsK R grow monotonically:
K1 ⊆K 2 ⊆ · · · ⊆ L
But lattice directions are finite
∃Rsat such that:
No new frequencydirectionsappear forR≥R sat
Only frequencymagnitudesincrease
Lord Sen, Shyamapada Mukherjee (National Institute of Technology, Rourkela)Expressive Power of QDRs February 20, 2026 9 / 19
Theorem: Saturation of Reachable Frequency Directions
Theorem 1 (Saturation of Reachable Frequency Directions).
LetS⊂Z d be the finite set of spectral vectors associated with the data-encoding
Hamiltonians, and define the integer lattice
L:= span Z(S).
ForR≥1, define:
S(R) :={s 1 +· · ·+sR |s i ∈S}, K R :=S (R) −S (R).
Then there exists a finite integerR sat such that for allR≥R sat:
1 Every lattice direction inLappears inK R
2 IncreasingRintroduces no new frequency directions, only larger magnitudes
Lord Sen, Shyamapada Mukherjee (National Institute of Technology, Rourkela)Expressive Power of QDRs February 20, 2026 10 / 19

cone(S) =

is apolyhedral cone
ByGordan’s Lemma:
The integer points in this cone form a finitely generated semigroup
There exists a finiteHilbert basisH={h 1, . . . , hm}
Eachh j is a finite sum of elements ofS
Key Insight:Once all basis directions are reachable, no new directions can appear.

Intuition Behind Saturation
QDRs generate Fourier modes on an integer lattice
Reuploading performs Minkowski sums on spectral vectors
Finite spectra⇒finite directional basis
Depth increasesradius, notdimensionality

Conclusions
QDRs computeband-limited Fourier series
Expressivity grows linearly with reuploads
Saturation limits functional diversity
Explains empirical plateaus and overfitting
Future work:
Residual QDR architectures
Variational action-based QDRs
